title: Animator
reason: Sora, Runway Gen-3, and Kling produce usable short clips but lack coherent long-form animation and character consistency. 50% task displacement (storyboarding, in-betweening, motion graphics) plausible in ~5y as models improve at multi-shot consistency.

title: Copywriter
reason: LLMs already handle bulk ad copy, email sequences, and product descriptions. Many agencies report 50%+ productivity gains. Short-form copywriting is already heavily AI-assisted; remaining value is brand voice and strategy.

title: Content Writer
reason: SEO articles, blog posts, and listicles are already majority AI-generated at scale. Goldman Sachs 2023 report flagged writing as among highest-exposure tasks. Human value remains in original reporting and expert opinion pieces.

title: Fashion Designer
reason: AI generates designs and mood boards effectively, but physical prototyping, fabric selection, fit testing, and trend-setting require embodied expertise. Industry adoption is slower due to craft culture and luxury brand resistance.

title: Film Director
reason: Directing requires creative vision, actor collaboration, real-time set decisions, and leadership. AI assists with pre-viz and post-production but cannot replace the human creative authority. Union protections (DGA) add further barriers.

title: Game Designer
reason: AI assists with level generation, balancing, and asset creation, but core game design (systems thinking, player psychology, fun factor) remains deeply human. Procedural generation aids but doesn't replace the designer's creative judgment.

title: Graphic Designer
reason: Midjourney, DALL-E 3, and Canva AI already automate logo variations, social graphics, and layout. McKinsey estimates high automation potential for routine design. Strategic brand work and client collaboration remain human for now.

title: Illustrator
reason: AI image generation has hit commercial quality for editorial and stock illustration. Custom character design and distinctive artistic styles still require human illustrators, but the volume of commodity illustration work is shrinking fast.

title: Influencer/Content Creator
reason: The product IS the human personality and authenticity. AI virtual influencers exist (Lil Miquela) but haven't displaced human creators. AI assists with editing and scheduling but the core value — human connection — resists automation.

title: Journalist
reason: AI handles data journalism, earnings summaries, and basic reporting (AP's Automated Insights). But investigative journalism, source cultivation, ethics judgment, and on-the-ground reporting remain human. WEF notes media jobs face moderate disruption.

title: Makeup Artist
reason: Entirely physical, hands-on work requiring client interaction, skin assessment, and artistic judgment in real-time. No viable robotic alternative exists or is near-term plausible. Frey-Osborne rates this very low automation probability.

title: Music Producer
reason: Suno, Udio, and AI mastering tools handle significant production tasks. But high-end production involves artist collaboration, sonic vision, and studio engineering that AI doesn't replicate. Mid-tier production work is already being disrupted.

title: Musician/Performer
reason: Live performance is the core revenue stream and is inherently human — audience connection, stage presence, improvisation. AI competes on recorded music but live music demand may actually increase as a uniquely human experience.

title: News Anchor
reason: AI avatars (Synthesia, Chinese state media AI anchors) can read scripts convincingly. However, trusted human faces still matter for credibility, breaking news judgment, and interviews. Smaller/digital outlets may adopt AI anchors faster.

title: Photo Retoucher
reason: AI tools (Adobe Firefly, Luminar AI, Topaz) already automate skin smoothing, background removal, color grading, and object removal. High-end fashion retouching still needs human finesse but the bulk of retouching work is already AI-assisted.

title: Photographer
reason: Photography requires physical presence, client direction, and creative eye. AI generates synthetic images but can't shoot weddings, events, or portraits. Stock photography is disrupted but commercial/event photography remains human-dependent.

title: Podcast Producer
reason: AI handles editing (Descript), transcription, show notes, and clip generation. But booking guests, editorial strategy, and audience development require human judgment. The production workflow is being heavily streamlined by AI tools.

title: Radio Broadcaster
reason: AI voice cloning and automated playlists can run basic radio formats. Personality-driven shows and live interaction remain human. Small/regional stations face faster displacement; major market talent is more protected.

title: Screenwriter
reason: LLMs draft competent scripts but lack originality, emotional depth, and cultural resonance. WGA 2023 contract set guardrails on AI use. AI assists with outlining and dialogue drafts but full screenwriting remains a human craft.

title: Social Media Manager
reason: AI handles content creation, scheduling, analytics, and basic community management. Tools like Jasper, Buffer AI, and ChatGPT cover most tactical work. Strategic brand voice and crisis management still need humans.

title: Sound Engineer
reason: AI mastering (LANDR, iZotope) handles post-production mixing. But live sound engineering (concerts, film sets) requires physical presence, acoustic judgment, and real-time problem-solving. Studio work faces faster disruption than live work.

title: Sports Journalist
reason: AI already generates game recaps and statistical analysis (AP, The Athletic use automated summaries). Feature writing, player interviews, and insider reporting remain human. Routine match reporting is the most vulnerable segment.

title: Tattoo Artist
reason: Entirely physical craft requiring steady hands on irregular body surfaces, pain management, client trust, and health/safety compliance. AI assists with design generation but the application is firmly human. No robotic tattoo system is commercially viable.

title: Technical Writer
reason: LLMs excel at generating API docs, user guides, and reference material from code and specs. GitHub Copilot and similar tools already auto-generate documentation. Human value remains in complex system architecture docs and user empathy.

title: Video Editor
reason: AI editing tools (Descript, Runway, CapCut AI, Adobe Premiere AI features) automate cuts, transitions, color grading, and rough edits. Creative storytelling and high-end editing remain human. Routine content editing is already heavily AI-assisted.

title: Voice Actor
reason: ElevenLabs, PlayHT, and similar tools produce near-indistinguishable voice clones. Audiobook narration, IVR, and e-learning voiceover are rapidly shifting to AI. Premium character acting and union-protected work (SAG-AFTRA) provide some buffer.

title: Advertising Executive
reason: AI handles creative generation, media buying optimization, and audience targeting. But client relationships, brand strategy, and creative direction require human judgment. Programmatic advertising is already heavily automated.

title: Branch Manager
reason: Physical presence required for staff management, local operations, and customer escalations. Banking branches are declining but remaining ones need human leadership. AI assists with reporting and scheduling but not the core management function.

title: Business Analyst
reason: AI excels at data analysis, report generation, requirements documentation, and process mapping. Goldman Sachs flags analyst roles as high-exposure to LLMs. Stakeholder interviews and change management remain human-dependent.

title: Business Manager
reason: Broad coordination role requiring human leadership, relationship management, and contextual judgment across departments. AI assists with analytics and reporting but the core management function is human.

title: CEO
reason: Strategic vision, stakeholder trust, board dynamics, public representation, and ultimate accountability are fundamentally human. AI becomes a powerful decision-support tool but does not replace the leadership role. McKinsey rates C-suite as low automation potential.

title: Customer Service Manager
reason: AI chatbots handle increasing volumes of Tier 1 support, reducing team sizes. But managing escalations, training staff, and maintaining service quality require human leadership. The team they manage shrinks but the role persists.

title: Event Planner
reason: Logistics, vendor management, on-site coordination, and client emotional support are heavily physical and interpersonal. AI assists with scheduling, budgeting, and vendor discovery but cannot execute events. Frey-Osborne rates event planning as low automation.

title: General Manager
reason: Broad operational oversight with heavy people management component. AI augments decision-making with analytics but physical presence, staff motivation, and cross-functional leadership remain human. Similar profile to business manager.

title: HR Manager
reason: AI handles resume screening, benefits administration, and policy Q&A. But employee relations, conflict resolution, sensitive conversations, and legal compliance judgment require humans. WEF notes HR faces moderate disruption.

title: Immigration Consultant
reason: Complex case-by-case regulatory judgment, document assembly, and client advocacy. AI assists with form preparation and regulation lookup but immigration law requires contextual interpretation and government liaison that resists automation.

title: Inventory Manager
reason: AI forecasting, demand planning, and automated reorder systems already handle most analytical inventory tasks. Amazon-style warehouse AI sets the benchmark. The remaining human role is exception handling and vendor negotiations.

title: Management Consultant
reason: McKinsey's own research acknowledges AI automates data analysis, benchmarking, and slide generation. But client relationship management, workshop facilitation, and organizational politics navigation remain human. Junior analyst work is most exposed.

title: Marketing Manager
reason: AI handles content creation, campaign analytics, A/B testing, and audience segmentation. Strategic positioning, brand building, and cross-functional leadership remain human. Tactical marketing execution is already heavily AI-augmented.

title: Migration Agent
reason: Similar to immigration consultant but with stronger regulatory licensing requirements (especially in Australia). Government interface, case advocacy, and complex application strategy resist automation. AI assists with form prep and compliance checking.

title: Operations Manager
reason: Process optimization is AI's strength, but operations management involves cross-functional coordination, crisis response, and staff management that require physical presence and human judgment. AI is a powerful augmentation tool here.

title: Procurement Manager
reason: AI handles spend analytics, supplier discovery, contract analysis, and purchase order automation. Supplier negotiations, relationship management, and strategic sourcing decisions remain human. Routine procurement is already heavily automated.

title: Product Manager
reason: AI assists with user research synthesis, roadmap analytics, and competitive analysis. But product vision, cross-functional leadership, user empathy, and stakeholder alignment are deeply human. The role may evolve but won't be displaced soon.

title: Project Manager
reason: AI handles scheduling, resource allocation, status reporting, and risk flagging. Tools like Monday AI and Asana AI automate significant workflow. Stakeholder management and team dynamics remain human but the role is shrinking in scope.

title: Property Manager
reason: Physical property inspections, tenant relations, maintenance coordination, and local regulatory compliance require on-site human presence. AI assists with rent collection, listing, and financial reporting but the core role is physical.

title: Public Relations Specialist
reason: AI generates press releases, media lists, and sentiment analysis. But crisis communications, journalist relationships, and strategic messaging require human judgment and trust. Routine PR writing is already AI-assisted.

title: Purchasing Agent
reason: Routine purchase orders, vendor comparison, and price tracking are highly automatable. E-procurement systems with AI already handle significant volumes. McKinsey flags procurement tasks as high automation potential.

title: Real Estate Agent
reason: AI handles property matching, market analysis, and virtual tours. But physical showings, negotiation, emotional support during major purchases, and local market knowledge require human agents. Redfin/Zillow AI augments but hasn't replaced agents.

title: Recruitment Consultant
reason: AI screening, sourcing, and matching are already mainstream (LinkedIn Recruiter AI, HireVue). But candidate relationship building, cultural fit assessment, and salary negotiation remain human. Volume recruiting is most exposed.

title: Regional Manager
reason: Multi-site oversight requiring travel, local adaptation, and distributed team leadership. AI assists with performance dashboards and reporting but the physical oversight and people management are firmly human. Low automation potential per McKinsey.

title: Retail Manager
reason: Physical store operations, staff management, and customer escalations require on-site presence. AI assists with inventory, scheduling, and analytics but the management function is human. Self-checkout reduces staff but someone still manages.

title: Sales Manager
reason: AI CRM tools handle pipeline analytics, lead scoring, and forecasting. But team motivation, key account relationships, and strategic deal-making require human leadership. The coaching and relationship aspects resist automation.

title: Scrum Master
reason: AI tools automate sprint planning, velocity tracking, and retrospective summaries. The facilitation and team coaching aspects remain human but many orgs question whether dedicated scrum masters are needed with AI-assisted project management.

title: Store Manager
reason: Similar to retail manager — physical presence, staff management, and operations. 8 years seems slightly high given AI scheduling and inventory tools, but physical store operations ensure the role persists. Amazon Go-style automation is still niche.

title: Sustainability Consultant
reason: Emerging and growing field with complex regulatory landscape (ESG reporting, carbon accounting). AI assists with data analysis and reporting frameworks but stakeholder engagement, policy interpretation, and strategy remain human-driven.

title: Baker
reason: Industrial baking is already automated but artisan/retail baking requires dough feel, oven judgment, and creative recipe development. Robotic baking systems exist for mass production but small bakery work remains manual. Reasonable timeline.

title: Barista
reason: Robotic baristas exist (Cafe X, Crown Coffee) but remain novelties. The social interaction, customization handling, and cafe atmosphere creation are core to the experience. Mass-market coffee chains may adopt robots faster than independent cafes.

title: Bartender
reason: Robotic bars exist on cruise ships and some novelty venues. But bartending is as much about social interaction, atmosphere, and personalized service as drink mixing. Cultural resistance to robot bartenders is strong in most markets.

title: Butcher
reason: Industrial meat processing has some automation but skilled butchery (custom cuts, customer consultation, whole-animal breakdown) requires physical dexterity and knowledge. Food safety regulations and variable animal anatomy limit robotic solutions.

title: Chef
reason: AI assists with recipe creation and kitchen management, but cooking is physical, creative, and requires taste/smell judgment. Moley robotic kitchen exists but costs $300K+ and handles limited recipes. Fine dining chefs are very safe.

title: Concierge
reason: AI chatbots handle information queries, booking, and recommendations effectively. Hotel concierge value is increasingly in personal touch and VIP service that AI cannot replicate. Budget hotels may replace with AI; luxury hotels won't.

title: Fast Food Cook
reason: Flippy (Miso Robotics) and similar handle frying/grilling. But full kitchen automation across prep, assembly, and cleaning isn't deployed at scale yet. High labor costs drive investment but rollout takes time. 2 years is aggressive — 4 is more realistic for 50% task coverage across the industry.

title: Florist
reason: Physical arrangement of delicate materials, creative design for occasions, seasonal knowledge, and client consultation. Online ordering reduces some florist roles but the physical craft is not automatable with current robotics.

title: Hotel Manager
reason: AI handles revenue management, booking optimization, and guest communications. But staff management, physical property oversight, guest escalations, and hospitality leadership require human presence. Similar to property manager with stronger service component.

title: Housekeeper
reason: Robotic vacuums exist but full room cleaning (beds, bathrooms, surfaces, restocking) in varied hotel/home environments is beyond current robotics. McKinsey estimates physical labor automation lags cognitive automation by years.

title: Pastry Chef
reason: Highly skilled physical craft requiring precision, creativity, and sensory judgment (texture, taste, appearance). More art than cooking. Industrial pastry production is automated but artisan work is robot-proof for the foreseeable future.

title: Restaurant Manager
reason: Physical presence for staff management, guest experience, kitchen coordination, and health/safety compliance. AI assists with scheduling, inventory, and reservations but the core management role requires a human on-site.

title: Sommelier
reason: AI wine recommendation apps exist but sommelier work is about tasting, service theater, cellar management, and guest relationship building. The sensory and hospitality elements are firmly human. A niche luxury role that resists automation.

title: Sous Chef
reason: Kitchen execution, team coordination, quality control, and physical cooking under pressure. Same physical barriers as chef but with more routine execution tasks. Industrial kitchen automation may affect institutional food service first.

title: Waiter/Waitress
reason: Robot servers exist in Asia (Pudu Robotics) for delivery but table service involves social interaction, upselling, problem-solving, and hospitality. QR code ordering reduces some tasks. Full replacement unlikely but task scope narrows over time.

title: Wedding Planner
reason: Highly emotional, relationship-driven work with complex logistics and day-of crisis management. AI assists with vendor discovery, budgeting, and checklists but the emotional support and physical coordination are fundamentally human.

title: Archaeologist
reason: Fieldwork, excavation, and contextual analysis require physical presence and expert judgment. AI assists with satellite imagery analysis, artifact classification, and data processing but the core field science is manual and interpretive.

title: Economist
reason: AI excels at data analysis, modeling, and forecasting. Goldman Sachs and McKinsey flag analytical roles as high-exposure. But policy judgment, theoretical innovation, and advisory work require human expertise. Research economist tasks are most exposed.

title: Environmental Scientist
reason: Mix of fieldwork (sampling, site assessment) and lab/data analysis. AI accelerates data processing and modeling but field collection and regulatory interpretation require human scientists. Moderate automation potential per McKinsey.

title: Forensic Scientist
reason: Lab analysis can be partially automated but chain of custody, expert testimony, crime scene processing, and evidence interpretation require trained humans. Legal system requirements for human expert witnesses provide strong protection.

title: Geologist
reason: Fieldwork (core sampling, site surveys, mapping) combined with data analysis. AI assists with seismic data interpretation and mineral exploration modeling but on-site geological assessment requires physical presence and expert judgment.

title: Lab Technician
reason: Routine lab tasks (sample processing, testing protocols, data recording) are increasingly automated with liquid handlers and automated analyzers. But equipment maintenance, troubleshooting, and non-standard procedures still need humans.

title: Marine Biologist
reason: Ocean fieldwork, diving, specimen collection, and ecosystem monitoring in remote environments. AI assists with species identification and data analysis but the physical field science in marine environments is among the hardest to automate.

title: Market Researcher
reason: AI handles survey design, data collection, statistical analysis, and report generation effectively. LLMs can synthesize qualitative research findings. McKinsey and Goldman Sachs flag research/analysis roles as high automation exposure.

title: Research Scientist
reason: AI accelerates hypothesis generation, literature review, data analysis, and even experimental design (AlphaFold, AI drug discovery). But novel experimental work, lab execution, and scientific creativity remain human. Varies greatly by field.

title: Survey Researcher
reason: AI handles survey design, distribution, response analysis, and insight generation. Tools like Qualtrics AI and SurveyMonkey AI automate most of the workflow. Human value remains in nuanced questionnaire design and stakeholder interpretation.

title: Statistical Assistant
reason: Data entry, basic statistical analysis, table generation, and report formatting are among the most automatable tasks. LLMs and tools like Code Interpreter handle these tasks already. Frey-Osborne rates clerical statistical work as 94% automatable.

title: Urban Planner
reason: AI assists with spatial analysis, traffic modeling, and zoning optimization. But community engagement, public hearings, policy negotiation, and balancing stakeholder interests are deeply human. Regulatory and democratic processes slow automation.

title: Athletic Trainer
reason: Hands-on physical assessment, injury rehabilitation, taping/bracing, and athlete relationship management. AI assists with performance analytics and injury prediction but the physical treatment and emotional support are firmly human.

title: Choreographer
reason: Creative movement invention, physical demonstration, working with dancers' bodies, and artistic vision. AI can generate motion capture sequences but cannot physically demonstrate or teach movement. Deeply embodied creative work.

title: Sports Coach
reason: AI provides performance analytics, opponent scouting, and training optimization. But player motivation, real-time tactical adaptation, physical demonstration, and team chemistry management require human leadership. Analytics augment but don't replace.

title: Head Coach
reason: Leadership, media management, game-day decisions under pressure, player development, and organizational politics. 8 years seems too aggressive — head coaching involves less routine work than the estimate implies. More aligned with 12 years.

title: Sports Scorekeeper
reason: Already largely automated via computer vision, sensor technology, and electronic scoring systems. Hawk-Eye, electronic line judges, and automated stats tracking have displaced most manual scorekeeping. Human scorekeepers persist mainly in amateur/local sports.

title: Video Game Tester
reason: AI testing tools (automated regression, pathfinding, bug detection) handle significant QA workload. But gameplay feel, UX judgment, and edge-case discovery still benefit from human testers. The split between automated and manual testing is shifting fast.

title: Museum Curator
reason: Scholarship, collection stewardship, exhibition design, community engagement, and institutional knowledge. AI assists with cataloging and research but curatorial vision and public programming are deeply human. Low automation per Frey-Osborne.

title: Diplomat
reason: Geopolitical negotiation, cultural sensitivity, representation, and trust-building between nations. AI assists with translation and briefing preparation but the interpersonal and political dimensions are fundamentally human. Among the most automation-resistant roles.

title: Interior Designer
reason: AI visualization and rendering tools (Midjourney, room planners) handle concept generation. But client relationships, spatial assessment on-site, material selection, and project management require human designers. Mid-range design work is most exposed.

title: Mining Worker
reason: Strong automation incentive due to danger and remote locations. Autonomous haul trucks (Caterpillar, Komatsu) already deployed in Australian mines. Rio Tinto operates fully autonomous mines. Underground mining is harder but surface mining automation is advancing rapidly.

title: Architect
reason: AI generative design tools produce building layouts and optimize structures. But client consultation, regulatory compliance, site visits, and creative vision for inhabited spaces require human architects. AI augments but doesn't replace the licensed professional.

title: Civil Engineer
reason: AI assists with structural analysis, modeling, and design optimization. But site inspections, regulatory compliance, public safety accountability, and professional liability require licensed human engineers. Professional licensing provides strong protection.

title: Translator
reason: AI translation (DeepL, Google Translate, GPT-4) handles most routine translation competently. Literary, legal, and diplomatic translation still needs humans for nuance and cultural context. Bulk translation work has already shifted heavily to AI with human post-editing.

title: UX Designer
reason: AI generates wireframes, prototypes, and UI designs (Figma AI, Galileo AI). But user research, empathy mapping, usability testing interpretation, and design strategy require human insight. Tactical UI work is most exposed; strategic UX is more protected.

title: Bank Teller
reason: ATMs, mobile banking, and AI chatbots have already displaced most teller functions. Branch transaction volumes have dropped 40%+ in most markets. Remaining tellers handle complex transactions and relationship banking. The role is already in steep decline.

title: Retail Sales Assistant
reason: Self-checkout, AI recommendations, and e-commerce reduce need for in-store sales help. But physical product assistance, fitting rooms, and customer service in specialty retail remain human. Mass retail is most exposed; luxury/specialty retail less so.
